fwd:cloudsec North America 2026 Scholarship
Washington DC · June 1–2, 2026

Scholarship Application

Why do you want to come to fwd:cloudsec?

As a Cloud Engineer building my expertise in DevSecOps, I'm intentional about learning how security truly operates in modern cloud environments. fwd:cloudsec stands out because it goes beyond surface-level success stories and dives into where cloud security breaks down — and how teams can improve. That honesty and technical depth are rare and incredibly valuable.

"I'm especially drawn to its unbiased, cross-cloud conversations comparing security strategies across platforms."

Those insights would directly strengthen my ability to design and advocate for resilient, secure systems in real-world environments. I'm also excited about connecting with other cloud security practitioners, learning from their experiences, exchanging ideas, and building relationships that continue beyond the conference.

Beyond personal growth, I'm committed to sharing what I learn with my community and mentees through knowledge sessions and practical guidance. This scholarship would make it possible for me to attend, engage meaningfully, and bring back insights that create impact far beyond the conference itself.

DevSecOps Cloud Security Community Impact

What do you hope to learn?

I hope to learn how cloud security actually works in real teams — not just in theory, but in the messy, real-world situations where things don't always go as planned. I'm especially interested in understanding where security efforts tend to fall short and how experienced engineers fix those gaps.

I want practical ideas I can apply immediately, whether that's improving how security fits into the development process or making cloud environments easier to monitor and protect.

I'm also looking forward to hearing honest lessons from other practitioners — what worked, what didn't, and what they would do differently.

Have you been to any other information security conferences?

Yes. Since 2023 I've attended several information security and cloud-focused conferences, including GITEX Nigeria and AfricaCyberFest. These events have exposed me to important conversations around cybersecurity trends, policy, and emerging threats within the African ecosystem.

I'm also actively involved in AWS Cloud Security West Africa, where I serve as a community leader. That role has allowed me not only to participate in discussions but also to help shape them — supporting knowledge sharing and community growth around cloud security in the region.

What career and/or degree are you pursuing?

I'm pursuing a career in Cloud Security with a strong focus on DevSecOps. My goal is to become a security-focused cloud architect who helps teams build secure systems from the start — rather than treating security as an afterthought.

I'm particularly interested in bridging the gap between engineering and security, making security practical, understandable, and embedded into everyday development workflows. Long-term, I see myself not only designing secure cloud environments but also mentoring others and contributing to the growth of cloud security communities — especially within Africa.

Cloud Architect DevSecOps Mentorship

What conference talk about cybersecurity/cloud fascinated you?

In the early days of my cloud journey, I attended my first AWS Cloud Security West Africa Conference in 2023 and listened to a talk on Zero Trust Architecture delivered by Precious Okwu, a Platform Engineer. That session genuinely shifted my perspective.

Before then, I saw security mostly as perimeter-based controls — but the talk broke down what it truly means to "never trust, always verify" in cloud environments.

It explained how identity, access control, and continuous validation play a central role in protecting distributed systems. That session sparked my deeper interest in cloud security and pushed me to start exploring how security can be intentionally built into cloud infrastructure from the ground up.

Zero Trust Identity & Access Cloud Infrastructure

Describe a cloud security project or learning experience you've pursued on your own.

One project I've pursued independently is designing a high-assurance key management system for a crypto blockchain company using AWS Nitro Enclaves. The goal is to protect blockchain private keys at the highest level possible by ensuring they never exist in plaintext on general-purpose servers.

Instead of storing keys directly on backend instances, all sensitive operations — wallet generation, transaction signing, and seed recovery — happen inside a Nitro Enclave: an isolated compute environment that even the parent EC2 instance cannot access. Communication between the application and the enclave happens securely over VSOCK, and each enclave generates an attestation document to cryptographically prove its identity.

I integrated AWS KMS with attestation-based policies so that encryption and decryption operations are only permitted if the request originates from a verified enclave.

Encrypted key material is stored as cipher blobs in DynamoDB, while build artifacts and logs are stored in S3. This project has significantly deepened my understanding of hardware-backed isolation, cryptographic trust boundaries, and designing systems where security is enforced by architecture — not assumption.

AWS Nitro Enclaves AWS KMS DynamoDB Cryptographic Trust Hardware Isolation
